summary: Fowler documents his procedure for squashing multiple Mercurial commits into a single commit using the mq (mercurial queues) extension. He was reorganizing files and folders, wanting to checkpoint his work along the way but present a clean single commit in the final history. The post walks through cloning repositories, importing commits as patches, folding them together, and pulling the result back. He notes this preserves file move history, which was important for his use case. The procedure involves multiple repository clones as a safety measure before editing history.
key_points: Mercurial encourages using multiple repositories where other systems would use branches - it's 'the Mercurial way of working'
Git-style diffs in Mercurial help track file moves properly, which is important for maintaining history across reorganizations
Cloning before editing history provides a safety net until you verify the squash worked correctly
Version control attention has evolved from audit (going back to old revisions) to collaboration to narrative (how a codebase changes over time)
money_quotes: Making a single commit makes it easier to understand what happened - particularly since moving files tends to complicate looking at repository logs.
It's important to clone at this point because I was about to edit history, so wanted to keep the original history handy until I knew it had worked.
Mercurial encourages different repositories where other systems, eg git or svn, would use different branches.
Early on the primary and only purpose was audit - to be able to safely go back to older revision - mainly to diagnose problems.
Now there's more attention to using them to provide a narrative of how a code base changes - hence the desire for history rewriting commands like this.
This need is built on top of the other two, but introduces new capabilities and new tensions.
key_insight: Version control has evolved beyond audit and collaboration to serve as a narrative tool for understanding how codebases change, creating new capabilities and tensions around history rewriting.
